ontology.foodon_gap_note: FOODON_03301638 represents carbonated water as a category; no Perrier-specific entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 AN0KL covers carbonated water generically; no brand-specific Perrier code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 11713004 is the closest parent for water-based beverages; no sparkling mineral water specific code confirmed.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 330 ml bottle of Perrier Sparkling Water, as commonly served in Singapore and cafes.

nutrient_source.title: Carbonated water, plain — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174831)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for plain carbonated water (seltzer/sparkling water) used as the nutrient basis. Perrier is a natural sparkling mineral water with negligible mineral content; macronutrient profile is identical to plain carbonated water. Sodium value cross-referenced with Perrier product label (approximately 9 mg per 100 ml) and confirmed against USDA Branded Foods entry for Perrier.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson et al.; water and non-caloric beverages convention
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure water and plain sparkling water contain no glycemic carbohydrates and therefore have no measurable glycemic index. By established convention in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI literature, non-caloric beverages with zero carbohydrate content are assigned GI = 1 (effectively zero) and classified as low GI. Glycemic load per 330 ml serving = 0.0 because carbohydrate_g = 0.

hypertension.guidance: Perrier Sparkling Water contains approximately 9 mg sodium per 100 ml, which is negligible and well within safe limits for individuals managing hypertension; it is a suitable hydration choice for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.guidance: Perrier Sparkling Water contains no sugar, no carbohydrates, and no calories, making it an ideal beverage for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as it does not raise blood glucose levels.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero fat, zero sodium burden, trace minerals
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Perrier Sparkling Water is free of fat, cholesterol, and added sodium, and its naturally occurring calcium and magnesium content may offer minor cardiovascular benefit; it is a heart-healthy hydration option.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Perrier Sparkling Water is a naturally carbonated mineral water sourced from a spring in Vergèze, in the Gard department of southern France. The water is naturally infused with carbon dioxide gas, which gives it its characteristic effervescence and crisp, refreshing taste. Perrier has a clean, slightly mineral flavour with a lively, persistent bubble structure that distinguishes it from artificially carbonated waters. It contains no calories, no sugar, no fat, no protein, and no carbohydrates, making it a zero-calorie beverage suitable for virtually all dietary patterns. Perrier is naturally rich in calcium (approximately 14.7 mg per 100g) and contains small amounts of magnesium (approximately 0.6 mg per 100g), contributing to daily mineral intake without adding caloric load. In Singapore, Perrier is commonly served in health-conscious restaurants, cafes, and fine-dining establishments as a premium table water alternative. It is appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease, as it does not raise blood glucose, does not contribute to sodium load, and contains no atherogenic fats. Perrier is certified halal, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is sold in iconic green glass bottles and is one of the most recognised sparkling mineral water brands globally.
common_user_queries: Can I drink Perrier if I have high blood pressure?
What minerals are in Perrier water?
Is sparkling water the same as Perrier?
How much sodium is in Perrier?

confidence_reason: Perrier Sparkling Water is a well-documented commercial product with a consistent and publicly available nutritional profile. Macronutrient values are confirmed via USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for plain carbonated water and cross-referenced with Perrier product labelling. Mineral values (calcium, magnesium, sodium) are confirmed from Perrier's published mineral analysis. GI is assigned by convention for zero-carbohydrate beverages per University of Sydney GI Database methodology.
notes: All core macros (energy_kcal, protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) are confirmed zero: energy_kcal is naturally zero as this is a non-caloric mineral water containing no macronutrients. protein_g is naturally zero as sparkling mineral water contains no protein by nature. fat_g is naturally zero as sparkling mineral water contains no fat by nature. carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as sparkling mineral water contains no carbohydrates by nature. sugar_g is naturally zero. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero. Confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174831). Sodium value of 9 mg per 100 ml is sourced from Perrier product mineral analysis and USDA Branded Foods. Calcium_mg per 100g is 14.7 mg, derived from Perrier's label stating 147 mg/L. Magnesium_mg per 100g is 0.6 mg, derived from Perrier's label stating 6 mg/L. These values are now correctly reflected in the per_100g section. iron_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the Perrier mineral analysis or USDA source for plain carbonated water. phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the Perrier mineral analysis or USDA source for plain carbonated water. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the Perrier mineral analysis or USDA source for plain carbonated water. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the Perrier mineral analysis or USDA source for plain carbonated water. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention. Ontology codes use closest_parent match type as no brand-specific Perrier entries exist in FoodOn, FoodEx2, or SNOMED CT. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the food is served in Singapore. This is a French-origin commercial product; no Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese local names are applicable.
